beats.prompt: You flip a fair coin over and over until HH shows up, and count the flips. Do it again for HT. On average, which one makes you wait longer?

options: They tie — both take 4 flips on average

Waiting for HH takes longer.note: Good instinct — we'll prove HH takes longer and pin down by exactly how much.

Waiting for HT takes longer.note: Let's test it — watch which near-miss throws your progress away.

They tie — both take 4 flips on average.note: This is the natural guess — but length isn't the whole story. A near-miss costs HH and HT differently, and that gap is exactly what we'll find.

hints: There's no wrong guess yet. Commit to one so you can test it against the math.
Most people pick the tie — it's the natural guess. Here's the thing to watch: both are length 2, but a near-miss doesn't cost the same for each.
You'll revisit this prediction once you've built the equations.

beats.prompt: Tap a pattern to preview its near-miss, then continue comparing both.

previews.HH: Matched one H, then flip a T: you fall all the way back to the start - no progress kept.
previews.HT: Matched one H, then flip another H: you keep a matched H and stay one flip away.

feedback.correct: Compare mode locked in: HH vs HT, same fair coin.
hints: Both patterns stay selected — this lesson is the HH vs HT contrast.
Length-3 patterns come later in Longer Patterns & Overlap.
Confirm compare mode to continue.

beats.prompt: Read the ½ refresher below, then continue.

interaction.title: ½ means 1 in 2
interaction.body: A fair coin is 50/50. "½" just means 1 out of 2 equally likely outcomes — heads or tails.

hints: Tap an outcome.
One of two equally likely.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Read what a 'state' is, then continue.

interaction.title: What a "state" is
interaction.body: A "state" is just how much of the pattern you've matched so far: none, one H, or done.

hints: Tap each state.
None, one H, or done.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Read the state graph guide below, then continue.

interaction.title: What a state graph is
interaction.body: This picture is a state graph. Each circle is a state — how much of HH you've matched so far: none, one H, or HH (the ringed circle = done). Each arrow is one coin flip: gold = heads, teal = tails. A flip moves you along an arrow — forward toward HH, back to the start, or looping in place.

hints: Each circle is a state.
Each arrow is a flip.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Press Flip and watch the state chip advance on the machine. Notice where progress goes after each flip.

feedback.correct: Notice the prefix-state chip: a near-miss sends you backward differently for HH than HT.
hints: Flip a few times and follow the active state chip.
Watch what happens right after a single H — that's the moment that matters.
Keep flipping until the guided replay reaches the near-miss, then continue.

beats.prompt: On the diagram, tap the destination state for each next flip (H and T), then press Check.

HH.correct: Right. For HH, a T after one H throws away all progress: E1 resets to E0.
hints: For HH you need two heads in a row. Does a tail keep any progress?
Look at the edge leaving E1 on T — it curves back below to the start.
E1 on T resets to E0. A tail erases the matched H entirely.

HT.correct: Right. For HT, an extra H after one H preserves progress: E1 self-loops back to E1.
hints: For HT, you still only need one H before a T. Does another H hurt you?
Look at the edge leaving E1 on H — it loops above onto itself.
E1 on H is a self-loop back to E1. You keep your matched H.

beats.prompt: HH and HT handle a near-miss differently. Read this note, then continue.

interaction.title: What just happened
interaction.body: A tail after one H throws HH all the way back to the start — but for HT, an extra H keeps your progress. Hold that thought: now we'll put a number on it.

hints: Reflect on the two near-misses.
One resets, one keeps progress.
Continue.

beats.prompt: Here's the finished equation for the start state (E0) — we did this one for you. Build the next state (E1) the same way: every flip costs 1 flip, and then the coin sends you to one of two places, half the time to each. Tap the pieces into the slots.

copy.workedExplanation: Start at E₀ (no H yet). One flip always costs 1. Then the coin splits: half the time an H sends you to E₁ (one H matched), half the time a T leaves you at E₀ (back to the start).

primer.title: Why E₀ can appear on both sides
primer.body: E₀ is a number we don't know yet — the average flips left from the start. It can show up on both sides because after a tail you're back at the start, just like x = 1 + ½x.

feedback.correct: That's the whole setup. See the 1/2 E0 piece in the E1 line? That's HH's catch: one wrong flip sends you all the way back to the start — which is exactly why HH takes longer.
hints: Each non-absorbing state starts with the 1 flip cost, then a 1/2 chance to each next state.
Look again at where E1 goes on a tail — that term should point at E0, not E1.

beats.prompt: Run a few simulations and watch the average settle toward a steady value, then continue.

interaction.title: Watch the average settle
interaction.body: "On average" means: if you ran this many times, the typical number of flips. Run it a few times and watch the running average settle toward a steady value.

feedback.correct: That settling value is the expected wait.
hints: Run several times.
The average steadies as runs grow.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Drag the slider to your best estimate of the average wait for HH, then lock it in.

feedback.correct: Locked. We'll mark your prediction on the chart and test it against simulation.
hints: Trust the equations you just built rather than the length-2 intuition.
Your marker will appear on the theory-vs-simulation chart next.
Move the slider and lock any value to continue.

beats.prompt: What is E[HH] — the average number of flips until HH? It's the value that makes its own equation balance. Slide the beam until the left side (E[HH]) equals the right side.

feedback.correct: E[HH] = 6. Substituting the absorbing state and solving gives the exact expected wait.
hints: Start with the part you already know: once you've seen HH, you're done — so its wait is 0. Build out from there.
Drop that 0 into the E1 line to get a number for E1. Then drop E1's number into the E0 line.
E1 works out to 4. Put it into E0 = 1 + 1/2 E1 + 1/2 E0; the two 1/2 E0 pieces combine, and you get E0 = 6 — about 6 flips, on average, to see HH.

beats.prompt: Run simulations and watch the empirical mean (the average of your runs) converge toward the theory line.

feedback.correct: The empirical mean settles near 6 — simulation confirms the recurrence.
hints: Run more simulations; the average steadies as the count grows — more trials means steadier, not 'due'.
6 is the average — most runs are shorter, a few are long. Your prediction marker stays fixed while the empirical line converges.
The theory line sits at 6 for HH; the empirical mean settles toward it.

beats.prompt: Here's why HH waits longer than HT: overlap is memory.

feedback.correct: HH's near-miss resets to zero; HT's near-miss preserves progress. That gap is the 2-flip difference.
hints: Compare the two highlighted edges leaving E1.
HH on T falls back to E0; HT on H stays at E1.
Near-misses that preserve progress shorten the expected wait.

beats.prompt: Optional: drag the coin bias p and watch the equations and expected times update live.

feedback.correct: Explore freely — biasing the coin stretches or shrinks both expected waits.
hints: This sandbox is exploratory and never graded.
Try p near 0 or 1 to see the waits diverge.
Skip or continue whenever you like.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: on a fair coin, what is E[HHH] - the average number of flips until three heads in a row first appear? Type a whole number.

interaction.scenario: You proved \(E[\text{HH}] = 6\) by building its state machine. \(\text{HHH}\) is the same idea, one state longer — any tail still wipes out all progress.

feedback.correct: \(E[\text{HHH}] = 14\). States \(E_0\) (no heads), \(E_1\) (one H), \(E_2\) (two H's); \(E_3 = 0\) since HHH is complete. Any tail resets to \(E_0\): \[ E_0 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0, \] \[ E_1 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0, \] \[ E_2 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0. \] Back-substitute: \(E_2 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0\) into \(E_1\) gives \(E_1 = \tfrac{3}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}E_0\); into \(E_0\): \(E_0 = \tfrac{7}{4} + \tfrac{7}{8}E_0\), so \(\tfrac{1}{8}E_0 = \tfrac{7}{4}\) and \(E_0 = 14\). Heads-run waits obey \[ E_k = 2E_{k-1} + 2, \] so \(E[\text{HH}] = 6 \to E[\text{HHH}] = 2 \cdot 6 + 2 = 14\) — each extra head doubles the wait and adds 2 for the full reset.
hints: Same machine as \(\text{HH}\), just one state longer. Any tail still sends you all the way back to the start.
From \(E_2 = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}E_0\) substitute upward: \(E_1 = \tfrac{3}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}E_0\), then \(E_0 = \tfrac{7}{4} + \tfrac{7}{8}E_0\), so \(\tfrac{1}{8}E_0 = \tfrac{7}{4}\) and \(E_0 = 14\).

beats.prompt: Reveal each recap card to review your prediction, the theory, the simulation, and the overlap insight.

feedback.correct: Look what you just did: you turned a coin question into a little machine, built its equations, and proved HH takes about 6 flips while HT takes 4 — by hand. That's the exact method professionals use.
hints: Overlap structure — not pattern length — sets the expected wait.
The 1/2 E0 reset term is what makes HH slower.
Next: transfer the method to THH vs HTH.
